topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a jarring lack of context. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously researched piece on urban planning, the one I’d poured months into, was attributed to 'Elias Vance.' My hands, which had been hovering over the 'send' button moments before, began to tremble. I reread the text, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. I clicked the link to the published article, my eyes scanning the familiar paragraphs, searching for the familiar cadence of my own argument, but finding only a slightly altered voice. A slow, deliberate breath escaped my lips. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. Who in the world was Elias Vance? I started typing a response, my fingers moving with an unnerving, almost robotic precision, every keystroke laced with a simmering disbelief.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the glow reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. The confirmation email felt like a physical blow. I had spent so much energy building this narrative, crafting every nuance, every supporting statistic. Now, it belonged to someone else. My jaw tightened, a hard line setting across my face. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palm, and turned it slowly in my hands. I walked over to the window, the city traffic a meaningless blur outside. A slow, almost imperceptible shake ran through my shoulders. This couldn't be right. I started pacing the small office, the sound of my footsteps echoing too loudly in the silence. I needed proof, something concrete to dismantle this phantom authorship.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I felt a sudden, profound stillness settle over me, followed by a rush of hot, indignant energy. My first reaction was to physically recoil from the monitor. I blinked rapidly, trying to force the information to recalibrate, to fit into a reality that stubbornly refused to conform. I leaned in close to the screen, squinting at the attribution. My breath hitched. It wasn't a typo; it was a complete misattribution. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening around my mouth signaled a deep internal churning. I pushed my chair back with a sharp scrape against the floor. I stood up, the movement stiff, and began to walk toward the door, my mind racing, cataloging every possible explanation, none of them settling the unsettling discrepancy.
